agent1_snippets.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Function -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Restorative material: Thanks to its easy placement and better marginal adaptation, glass ionomer cement is popularly used in pediatric restorations. GIC is also indicated for restoring permanent teeth in low stress-bearing areas like class III and V lesions and is the material of choice in patients with a high risk of caries due to the release of fluoride. Luting agent: GIC can lute indirect restorations (metal and metal-ceramic), post, core, and orthodontic bands, and brackets. [4] [8] Pulp protection: GIC is used as a liner or base beneath metallic and composite restorations (sandwich technique). [9]

agent1_snippets.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Technique -- Root Canal Sealers
agent1_snippets.content: Several techniques are used to insert the sealer into the root canal and ensure it evenly coats the prepared surface. One technique utilizes a screw-like file that attaches to a slow-speed handpiece called lentulo spiral. [36] The lentulo spiral is dipped into the sealer and inserted into the root canal. When the handpiece is activated, the lentulo spiral spins and evenly distributes the sealer on the walls through centrifugal force. [37] Its use has several downsides, including the risk of instrument fracture due to the instrument being very fragile and easily breakable. [38] [36] Additionally, it has been shown to accelerate the setting of resin-based sealers due to the stirring effect. [19] Other popular techniques for inserting sealer include the use of gutta-percha cones. The operator butters the pre-fit paper point or gutta-percha cone in the sealer and then inserts it in the canal using a pumping action to ensure even coating. [1]

agent1_snippets.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedure
agent1_snippets.content: Place orthodontic separators between the contact point of the primary molar for 4 or 5 days to create room for the crown. Select the smallest-sized crown covering all the cusps, approaching the contact points, and giving a 'spring back' effect. Adjustments of the crown are to be made using band-forming pliers if necessary. Cementation of the crown with glass ionomer cement. The tooth must be maintained in occlusion until the cement sets, followed by excess removal using floss. [25] [26] The Hall technique should be chosen as an alternative when the traditional approach with crown preparation is implausible. [25]

agent1_snippets.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Cementation
agent1_snippets.content: A luting cement should be used to secure the crown. Once the crown is seated on the prepared tooth, it should be kept under pressure while the cement hardens. Manufacturers recommend glass ionomer, zinc polycarboxylate, and zinc phosphate cement but fluoride-leaching cement has additional advantages. Due to the clinical advantages, RMGIC is the preferred choice. [22] However, research suggests the type of cement used has little effect on retention, with the essential retentive components derived from proper crown contouring and crimping. Operators must pay deliberate attention to the removal of excess cement. This is normally accomplished by running a pointed instrument around the cemented crown's margins and sliding knotted dental floss buccolingually through the contact areas after the cement setting. Excess cement has been shown to affect gingival health. [8] [6]

agent1_snippets.title: Mandible Reconstruction -- Technique or Treatment
agent1_snippets.content: Before plating, the jaw should be reduced to its native position; the wear facets of any teeth present should be appropriately opposing to make sure the jaw is set in its pre-traumatic or pre-operative configuration. Temporary maxillary-mandibular fixation can be achieved with screws and 24 gauge wires, arch bars, and other methods. If the area being reconstructed has comminuted fractures, then the bone fragments should be removed, and the wound irrigated before reconstruction. If a section of the mandible is missing, then the remaining bony edges should be freshened to improve healing; a reciprocating saw can be used to remove an additional 1-2 mm of bone until fresh, bleeding edges are seen.

agent1_snippets.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
agent1_snippets.content: The Benex device (Benex, Lucerne, Switzerland) is the most frequently cited vertical extraction system on the market today. However, some notable disadvantages of the Benex system are that the technique is time-consuming, and its mechanism is achievable only by counterbalancing against the dental arch, placing adjacent teeth and alveolar bone at risk for accidental fracture. [25] Additionally, the system is costly when compared to more conventional armamentaria. For these reasons, vertical extraction systems may not be the most practical means for the atraumatic extraction of teeth and roots. On the contrary, some have suggested a more cost-effective alternative to the Benex extraction system using a similar variation of the vertical extraction principle used by Benex. The technique requires using a miniplate screw fixated within the radicular canal and with a wire wound under the head of the screw. The wire-screw-root combination extrudes coronally using a wire twister and the nearby palatal bone as the fulcrum. [27] [28]

agent1_snippets.title: Dental Implants -- Technique or Treatment
agent1_snippets.content: Surgical protocols: There are three surgical approaches which are in use over the years: (1) two-stage (2) one-stage, and (3) immediate-loading. The two-stage surgical procedure first places the implant body below the soft tissue until the bone begins to heal (usually 2 to 3 months for mandible and 3 to 6 for maxilla). During the second stage of surgery, soft tissues are reflected to attach a permucosal element or abutment. In one-stage surgical approach, the implant body in the bone and the permucosal element above the soft tissue are both placed simultaneously until initial bone maturation has occurred. The abutment of the implant then replaces the permucosal element without the need for a secondary soft tissue surgery. The immediate-restoration approach places the implant body and the prosthetic abutment at the initial surgery, and restoration (mostly transitional) is then attached to the abutment.

agent1_snippets.content: Absolute dental contraindications include a hopeless dentition that is better treated with tooth removal and complete denture therapy. agent1_snippets.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Buccal/Lingual Reduction
agent1_snippets.content: Reducing the buccal and lingual surfaces is the third step in the preparation. The reduction of the buccal and lingual mucosa is optional. Some authors suggested preparing the buccal and lingual walls to create a gingivally inclined long bevel to make crown placement easier. On the other hand, others advocated for none to minimal (0.5 to 1 mm) preparation on the vestibular and lingual aspect of the crown, unless pronounced enamel convexity should only be reduced by a small amount. [12] Duggal and Curzon recommended trying the selected crown before lingual and buccal reduction. [13] Lastly, the operator should round all line and point angles. Occlusal-lingual and occlusal-buccal angles are beveled at 45 degrees. Bur is held parallel to the tooth’s long axis, and one should carefully blend the surfaces. Remove any remaining caries and perform appropriate pulp therapy if pulp involvement is present. The rubber dam should be left in place until cementing the crown. If it interferes with the seating and adaptation of the crown, it can be removed after tooth preparation. [6]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
agent2_snippets.content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 120
agent2_snippets.content: Iron antianemic preparations are organized into three pharmacologic sub‑categories: “Iron bivalent, oral antianemic preparations” (ferrous Fe²⁺ salts such as iron ascorbate, ferrous fumarate, ferrous asparto‑glycinate, polysaccharide‑bound iron complex), “Iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations” (ferric Fe³⁺ salts such as ferric pyrophosphate and sodium iron‑edetate), and “Iron in other combinations,” which groups formulations that blend iron with additional nutrients. All three sub‑categories are incorporated into the broader therapeutic class “IRON/MULTIVITAMINS,” defined as multivitamins with minerals that combine elemental iron with a spectrum of vitamins, trace elements, and adjunctive agents to treat iron‑deficiency anemia while supporting overall nutritional status. Representative IRON/MULTIVITAMINS products include bariatric‑specific chewable tablets (“BARIATRIC MULTIVITAMINS W/IRON TAB, CHEWABLE”), liquid drops (“MULTIVITAMINS W/IRON DROPS, ORAL”), vitamin‑B‑complex

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1738
agent2_snippets.content: IRON/MULTIVITAMINS is a multivitamin and mineral preparation that includes iron and a broad spectrum of vitamins and nutrients. It is classified as a multivitamin agent and is regarded as a specific type of Multivitamin+mineral preparations, while also being listed as a member of the broader group Multivitamins with minerals. The product line encompasses numerous ingredient combinations, such as MULTIVITAMINS W/IRON DROPS, ORAL; VITAMIN B COMPLEX W/C & IRON CAP; and a capsule formulation containing folic acid 1 MG, polysaccharide iron complex 150 MG, and vitamin B12 0.025 MG. Additional variants include multivitamin products that pair iron with a vitamin B complex and vitamin C, with folic acid, with essential fatty acids, or with vitamin B12, as well as formulations that combine iron with vitamins A, D, and C in oral liquid form. A specific formulation, Multi‑Day with Calcium and Extra Iron, represents a narrower relationship within this family. Together, these relationships illustrate that IRON/MULTIVITAMINS provides comprehensive supplementation of iron alongside B‑vitamins, vitamin C, folic acid, and other essential nutrients, supporting patient care where iron deficiency and multinutrient supplementation are indicated.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2272
agent2_snippets.content: IRON/MULTIVITAMINS is classified as a multivitamin‑plus‑mineral preparation and encompasses a broad range of formulations that combine iron with various vitamins and nutrients. The product line includes specific items such as “MULTIVITAMINS W/IRON DROPS, ORAL” and “VITAMIN B COMPLEX W/C & IRON CAP,” each of which contain iron together with vitamin B complexes, vitamin C, folic acid, and vitamin B12 in oral dosage forms. Additional formulations are described as “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral capsule,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet,” and “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with Folic Acid, Iron, and Essential Fatty Acids oral capsule,” highlighting the inclusion of essential fatty acids alongside the vitamin‑iron blend. Other variants emphasize vitamin A, D, and C combined with iron in an oral liquid, and a calcium‑enriched version labeled “Multi‑Day with Calcium and Extra Iron,” which represents a narrower, calcium‑focused subcategory within the broader IRON/MULTIVATIMINS family. Across these products, the common therapeutic intent is to provide supplemental iron together with a spectrum of B‑group vitamins, vitamin C, folic acid, vitamin B12, and additional micronutrients (such as essential fatty acids, calcium, vitamin A, and vitamin D) in convenient oral forms—including capsules, tablets (including extended‑release), drops, and liquids—to support iron repletion and overall nutritional status.
